prompt: Verify that the ORCHESTRATOR PUSHBACK (max 3 rounds) hard rule actually works end-to-end. Spawn a Codex plan reviewer pane, run the initial review (round 0), then drive 3 pushback rounds with specific concerns per the rocky skill's Phase 2.5.3.5 pattern. Each round must produce a distinct response from Codex that directly addresses the concern. After round 3, the orchestrator escalates to the user.
expected_behavior: 4 review files (r0/r1/r2/r3) all written, each addressing a different specific concern, each building on the previous round's content. Demonstrates that the pushback loop is implementable end-to-end on top of the swarm bash transport.
test_method: Driven manually by the parent orchestrator (not a subagent) to keep tight control over the round timing and prompt content. Each pushback prompt followed the rocky skill's Phase 2.5.3.5 template: 'Round R/3 — pushback: I am not assured. Specific concern: [exact issue]. Re-read [target], address this specific concern, and update your review at [path].'
assertions: Codex pane spawned and stayed alive across all 4 turns (r0 + r1 + r2 + r3)
Round 0 produced a NEEDS_REVISION review listing multiple ambiguities
Round 1 pushback ('propose concrete recommendations per ambiguity') produced a NEW review with concrete proposals for each ambiguity
Round 2 pushback ('verification recipe for idempotent migration') produced a NEW review with a concrete shell verification command
Round 3 pushback ('cross-task dependency consistency') produced a NEW review picking ONE stack for both tasks with explicit field-mapping contracts
All 4 review files are distinct (different sizes, different content)
Each round was responsive to its specific concern (not a copy-paste of the previous round)
Each round built on the previous round's content (Codex maintained context)
The pane was killed cleanly on completion
After round 3, escalation to the user would fire per the hard rule
